questions.question: What is transpilation in the context of quantum hardware?

options.text: Compiling circuits for specific hardware constraints

questions.explanation: Transpilation compiles ideal circuits to run on specific hardware, handling gate decomposition, qubit routing, and optimization.

questions.question: Why might a circuit need SWAP gates after transpilation?

options.text: To route operations between non-adjacent qubits

questions.explanation: Hardware has limited connectivity. SWAPs move qubit states to positions where required two-qubit gates can be performed.

questions.question: What typically happens when you submit a job to quantum cloud?

options.text: It enters a queue and waits for execution

questions.explanation: Cloud quantum computers have queues. Jobs wait their turn based on factors like priority, system availability, and backend selection.

questions.question: Why do hardware results differ from simulator results?

options.text: Hardware has gate errors, measurement errors, and decoherence

questions.explanation: Real hardware has imperfections: gate errors (~0.1-1%), measurement errors (~1-5%), decoherence, and crosstalk cause deviations from ideal results.

questions.question: Which cloud service provides access to multiple quantum hardware vendors?
questions.explanation: Amazon Braket provides access to IonQ (trapped ions), Rigetti (superconducting), QuEra (neutral atoms), and more through one interface.

questions.question: What is error mitigation?

options.text: Classical post-processing to reduce noise effects

questions.explanation: Error mitigation uses classical techniques (like ZNE, measurement error correction) to reduce the impact of noise on results without full error correction.

questions.question: Running on quantum hardware is free on all platforms.
questions.explanation: False. While IBM Quantum offers a free tier, most cloud quantum computing has costs. Amazon Braket charges per-shot, and premium access requires payment.
